result:
The rain is falling in that quiet, persistent way that makes the world feel small and close. It’s the kind of afternoon that feels suspended in amber, where the only sound is the soft percussion on the roof and the distant, hollow gurgle of a downspout. I find myself thinking about mushrooms.

Not the ones you buy at the store, but the others. The ones that appear overnight in the damp, dark corners of the lawn, pushing aside pine needles and leaf litter with a silent, alien force. They are organisms of profound patience and sudden action. For weeks, months, the mycelium threads itself through the dark soil, a vast, hidden network—a secret internet of the forest floor. And then, when conditions are just right, it sends up a fruiting body. A mushroom. It’s a signal flare. A whispered declaration: *I am here. I have been here all along.*

There’s a comfort in that, I think. The idea that beneath the surface of what we see—the neat lawns, the paved paths, the busy schedules—there is this sprawling, connected, patient life. It’s working its slow alchemy, breaking down the old to make way for the new, communicating through chemical signals we can’t perceive. It doesn’t care for our calendars or our anxieties. It operates on a time scale of decay and renewal, of dampness and darkness and sudden, glorious emergence.

I’ve been reading about the "Wood Wide Web," the way trees use fungal networks to share resources, to send warnings about pests, to nourish their sick or shaded neighbors. An ancient, cooperative socialism, conducted in sugar and warning signals. It contradicts the story we’re often told about nature being purely "red in tooth and claw." Here, in the dark, there is also barter. There is community. There is support.

It makes me look at my own life differently. What are my hidden networks? Not the digital ones, buzzing with frantic notifications, but the quiet, sustaining ones. The friend I can call after a year of silence and pick up as if no time has passed. The memory of my grandmother’s hands, how they felt, guiding mine as we kneaded dough. The collective, unspoken understanding in a room when someone shares a true, vulnerable thing. These are my mycelium. They thread under the surface of my days, often forgotten, but they are what hold the soil of my self together. They are what, when the conditions of grief or joy become just right, allow something unexpected to fruit—a moment of clarity, a burst of creativity, a deep, resilient peace.

The rain is easing now. A soft, gray light is seeping back into the sky. The mushroom in the yard, a perfect, ivory dome beaded with rain, will probably be gone in a day or two. Its work is brief and spectacular. But underneath, the network remains. It’s always there, in the dark, doing its quiet, essential work of connection.

And maybe that’s enough to think about on a rainy afternoon. That we, too, are part of networks seen and unseen. That our quiet, subterranean connections are what truly sustain us. And that every so often, it’s okay—beautiful, even—to send up a signal flare. To fruit. To say, “I am here. I have been here all along.” And then to settle back, knowing the connection persists, even when we can’t see it.
